entities.description: The unique known surviving copy of Caxton's c. 1489 print. It is incomplete: the fifth preliminary leaf, the leaf after Bii, and all text after Miiii are missing; later inserted matter includes a handwritten replacement table leaf.

holding.notes: Part of the University of Manchester Library Early Printing Collection; previously in the Spencer library.

notes: Printed in Caxton type 6 with woodcut initials.
The current digital object has 210 canvases because it includes every side of original and inserted leaves plus binding and flyleaves.

entities.description: The physical French book that Caxton says he had earlier sold to Margaret Beaufort and that she returned with a request that he translate it into English. Its present identity and location are not established.

next_steps: Consult Rosa Anna Greco's 2002 edition and stemma, Hudson's complete textual notes, and manuscript provenance scholarship.
Do not merge the lost exemplar with BnF Français 24371 without codicological provenance rather than textual similarity alone.
openQuestions.question: Was Caxton's physical French exemplar one of the surviving long-prose manuscripts, and what precise stemmatic relationship does it have to BnF Français 24371?

next_steps: Ask U-M Digital Collections to reconcile the exact item's current rights statement with the legacy availability text.

reviewNotes: The abstract Caxton work, its lost French exemplar, two extant source objects, Goodwine adaptation, Kellner editions, U-M encoding, modern Hudson edition, digital reproductions, and repository artifact are modeled separately.

The Manchester cache contains the complete 210-canvas bound object. Caxton's work is mapped to canvas labels 14–204, while later inserted leaves, blanks, flyleaves, and binding views remain in the full bundle.
The BnF cache contains all 115 canvases of Français 24371. Visual inspection maps the long prose romance to canvases f2–f111 and leaves binding and flyleaf views intact.

Manchester's exact item metadata and provider policy apply CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 to its images; Gallica permits free attributed noncommercial reuse but requires a paid license for commercial reuse. Local caching does not authorize redistribution.

The current U-M item says Public Domain / No Copyright–United States, while the 1997 XML header contains a restrictive legacy availability statement. Both are preserved pending provider clarification.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a local version of University of Michigan CME item Blanchardyn, keyed from the 1962 reprint of Leon Kellner's 1890 EETS edition. Kellner transcribed the unique incomplete Caxton print now Manchester Incunable 15027, supplied its missing internal leaf and French comparison text from BnF Français 24371, and used the Huntington's 1595 Goodwine adaptation for parallel specimens, the continuation after the Caxton copy breaks off, and an appendix. Caxton's c. 1489 Middle English work translates and adapts a now-unidentified physical copy of the long Burgundian French prose Blancandin. Complete public IIIF bundles are cached locally for the full 210-canvas Manchester bound volume and all 115 canvases of BnF Français 24371, with exact work ranges recorded inside each full object.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous French prose adapter; translated and adapted by William Caxton
catalogSummary.title: Blanchardyn and Eglantine

catalogingSubject.label: Blanchardyn and Eglantine
catalogingSubject.scope_note: William Caxton's c. 1489 Middle English prose translation and adaptation of the long French prose Blancandin et l'Orgueilleuse d'Amours, including Caxton's dedication to Margaret Beaufort, table, and fifty-four-chapter romance. This intellectual work is distinct from its lost French exemplar, the unique incomplete Manchester print, Leon Kellner's composite 1890 edition and 1962 reprint, Thomas Pope Goodwine's 1595 refashioning, the University of Michigan encoding, and the repository XML artifact.

contentParts.label: Caxton's dedication to Margaret Beaufort
contentParts.summary: Caxton presents the translated book to Margaret Beaufort, explains that she returned the French book he had sold her and requested an English translation, and commends noble histories as instruction and recreation.

contentParts.label: Table of fifty-four chapters

locators.value: image resources page14–page20; page20 is a later handwritten supply

contentParts.summary: A chapter table naming Blanchardyn and Eglantine and outlining the complete intended narrative, including material now missing from the unique print.

contentParts.label: Romance narrative

locators.value: image resources page24–page203 for the surviving print, ending at sig. Miiii verso
pp. 11–223, with supplied French and 1595 passages explicitly marked

contentParts.summary: Fifty-four chapters following Blanchardyn's education, adventures, love of Eglantine, alliance with Sadoyne, campaigns, rescues, marriages, and accession.

notes: The normalized range expresses the approximation and is not evidence for exact beginning and ending years.

extent.description: The Caxton table enumerates fifty-four prose chapters. The repository XML BODY has one dedication, one table, and fifty-two DIV1 chapter containers because chapters V–VII share a container; its 512 L elements include editorial/source-comparison verse and brief embedded inscriptions or quotations, none of which makes the prose romance mixed-form.

formStatements.description: The romance, dedication, and chapter table are prose. Brief embedded verse inscriptions or quotations, together with verse in Kellner's introduction and French comparisons, do not constitute a second substantive whole-work form.

genres.label: Didactic romance
notes: Hudson describes it as pedagogical; Caxton's dedication commends noble histories as instruction in chivalry and constancy.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: French and modern English passages in Kellner's edition and the U-M encoding belong to editorial and comparison layers, not to the whole cataloged Caxton work.

The XML's LANGUSAGE lists French and Latin but omits Middle English; direct inspection of the Caxton text, Manchester catalog, and modern edition establishes Middle English as the cataloged work's primary language.

The repository XML's 419 PB milestones, 512 L elements, sixty total DIV1 elements, and editorial front/back matter describe Kellner's composite volume and encoding, not the intrinsic extent or mixed form of Caxton's romance.
Dates, rights, and access conditions for the 1489 print, 1595 print, 1890 edition, 1962 reprint, modern digital editions, and manuscript images remain attached to those separate lineage layers.

next_steps: Consult Greco's 2002 stemma, Hudson's full textual notes, and provenance studies of the long prose manuscripts.
Preserve the lost exemplar and BnF Français 24371 as separate entities unless codicological provenance proves identity.
openQuestions.question: Can Caxton's lost French exemplar be identified more precisely, and was it physically identical with any surviving long-prose manuscript rather than merely textually close to BnF Français 24371?

next_steps: Review current ISTC/GW dating arguments, paper and type evidence, and the chronology of Margaret Beaufort's commission.
Do not convert the approximate print date into an exact composition date without new evidence.
openQuestions.question: Can Caxton's translation and printing be dated more narrowly than c. 1489?

next_steps: Compare Kellner's French and Goodwine supplies with Hudson's new English translation of BnF Français 24371 and her textual notes.
Keep supplied text visibly scoped in future editions rather than presenting it as surviving Caxton wording.
openQuestions.question: How much of Caxton's exact wording can be reconstructed for the missing internal leaf and lost ending, as distinct from reconstructing the narrative of his French source?

notes: The printing place is not promoted to a separately proven place of translation.

scope.description: Authorship and adaptation of the long French prose source narrative translated by Caxton
Middle English version with additions, expansions, altered emphases, and an English dedication
Immediate composite source edition published in 1890 and reprinted in 1962
Transcription of the unique Caxton print with explicitly marked supplied passages
Editorial comparison apparatus across the edition, drawing on BnF Français 24371 and the 1595 Goodwine print

subjects.label: Christian-Muslim conflict and conversion
notes: The label describes the romance's represented conflict and conversion episodes, not the identities of historical peoples or modern readers.

summaries.value: Kept ignorant of warfare despite his royal birth, Blanchardyn learns of chivalry from Trojan images and secretly leaves Frisia to seek adventure. He wins renown in combat and the love of Eglantine, the Proud Lady in Love, but repeated sieges, captures, shipwrecks, political betrayals, and campaigns separate them. With his companion Sadoyne, Blanchardyn rescues family and allies, defeats Alymodes and Subyon, returns to Eglantine, marries her, and becomes king.

The Caxton work consists of a dedication to Margaret Beaufort, a table, and fifty-four prose chapters. Its plot moves through three broad phases: Blanchardyn's chivalric formation and encounter with Eglantine; campaigns, capture, shipwreck, and alliance with Sadoyne; and the paired rescues and final marriages. The unique surviving print loses one internal leaf and everything after signature Miiii, so later editors reconstruct those portions from French and later English sources.

The source XML represents Kellner's edition rather than a pure continuous copy of Caxton's surviving print: early chapters include parallel passages from the 1595 English adaptation, the internal lacuna is supplied from BnF Français 24371, and the ending combines the French witness with Goodwine. Those editorial supplements are lineage layers, not alternate intrinsic forms or languages of the cataloged Caxton work.

notes: The unique print has no original title page; this established title is supported by the table and modern catalogs and editions.
A normalized cataloging form shared with later English adaptations; it does not make Goodwine's 1595 version identical to Caxton's work.

titles.value: Here begynneth the table of the victoryous prynce Blanchardyn, sone of the noble kyng of Fryse, and of Eglantyne Quene of Tormaday

scope.description: Title of Kellner's composite source edition rather than an original title of Caxton's work

titles.value: Caxton's Blanchardyn and Eglantine, c. 1489: From Lord Spencer's Unique Imperfect Copy, Completed by the Original French and the Second English Version of 1595

work.author: Anonymous French prose adapter; translated and adapted by William Caxton

publication.embeddedTitle: Caxton's Blanchardyn and Eglantine c. 1489 — FROM LORD SPENCER'S UNIQUE IMPERFECT COPY, COMPLETED BY THE ORIGINAL FRENCH AND THE SECOND ENGLISH VERSION OF 1595,

work.summary: Kept ignorant of warfare despite his royal birth, Blanchardyn learns of chivalry from Trojan images and secretly leaves Frisia to seek adventure. He wins renown in combat and the love of Eglantine, the Proud Lady in Love, but repeated sieges, captures, shipwrecks, political betrayals, and campaigns separate them. With his companion Sadoyne, Blanchardyn rescues family and allies, defeats Alymodes and Subyon, returns to Eglantine, marries her, and becomes king.
